results.possibleIdentity: Dwight Winton Berreman (born May 30, 1928) is a prominent American optical physicist who had a long and distinguished career at Bell Laboratories in Murray Hill, New Jersey, where he served as a Member of Technical Staff. He is widely recognized for his pioneering contributions to the field of liquid crystals and display technology. In 1972, he introduced the 4×4 transfer-matrix method, a foundational computational tool used to simulate the linear optics of layered anisotropic media, which became essential for modeling liquid crystal displays (LCDs). He is also the namesake of the 'Berreman effect' (1972), which explains the alignment of liquid crystals on surface-treated substrates based on elastic strain energy. Earlier in his career, in 1962, he invented the 'gas lens,' a device using thermal gradients in gases to focus laser beams. His expertise encompassed a broad range of technical fields, including X-ray optics, spectroscopy, color theory, and computer-generated graphics and film for scientific visualization. Berreman's interdisciplinary work at Bell Labs during the late 1960s and 1970s aligned with the collaborative ethos of the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) movement, which drew heavily on the expertise of Bell Labs engineers for projects involving computer graphics and experimental media. He was a resident of Westfield, New Jersey, and received the Jan Rajchman Prize from the Society for Information Display in 2011 for his analytical work on electro-optic effects.
